posts.name: Ph!l M@rx
posts.cooked:
Going to dinner at a nice local byob tonight and looking for recommendations of a type (not specific bottle) of wine to pair with this dish. Based on a recent thread, I was thinking about a Nebbiolo, but open to any suggestions.

Tajine D’agneau

Lamb shank braised with tomato and north African spices, with spring peas, zucchini, chic peas, and apricot, over couscous, garnished with harissa and yogurt
Top Blaufränkisch from Austria , or Cote-Rotie (like La Landonne/Rostaing), both around 2010

posts.name: Sarah Kirschbaum
posts.cooked: A full bodied rosé, one with some grip and tannin, not provencal style.

posts.name: Sam Kwak
posts.cooked: Second the recommendation for a northern Rhone. Could see it going well with garnachas from central Spain or mencias from Bierzo also.

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked: Musar would work well too most likely.

posts.name: Sarah Kirschbaum
posts.cooked: If not my above suggestion, I agree Cote Rotie is the correct red. The viognier has the apricot notes.

posts.name: Doug Schulman
posts.cooked:
Between the spices, apricots, and harissa, I think red wine would clash terribly with this. I would go with a Pinot Gris or Gewurztraminer from Alsace, something ripe and off-dry.

I also like the bold rosé idea, or off-dry Vouvray.

posts.name: H 3n ry  Br 1m 0
posts.cooked: McLaren Vale Grenache

posts.name: Sarah Kirschbaum
posts.cooked: I was just about to edit my post to add Alsace.for a white, though the chromatically challenged will always insist on red.

posts.name: Ari Tzikas
posts.cooked: Greek Xinomavro. Thymiopoulos, Kir Yianni or Alpha Estates.

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked: Made me think of the “bigger” Pibarnon Rosé Nuances.

posts.name: Bill Johnson
posts.cooked: A youngish Châteauneuf-du-Pape.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked: A dish with pretensions that needs to be thumped into submission. Nothing light will work, you need a heavyweight Rhone, a Joe Frazier of Chateauneufs, with a touch of sweetness and massive amounts of alcohol.

posts.name: Jim Stewart
posts.cooked: Alain Graillot ‘Syrocco’ Syrah (from Morocco)

posts.name: Ph!l M@rx
posts.cooked: A young Napa Cab it is then!!!

posts.name: Ph!l M@rx
posts.cooked: Thanks for the suggestions- searching my cellar tracker inventory for potential candidates…

posts.name: Sarah Kirschbaum
posts.cooked:
Mark_Golodetz:

A dish with pretensions that needs to be thumped into submission. Nothing light will work, you need a heavyweight Rhone, a Joe Frazier of Chateauneufs, with a touch of sweetness and massive amounts of alcohol.

Huh. In this house, the worst thing we can say about a pairing choice is that it’s "pairing by submission.:
I like the N Rhone idea too

posts.name: Brent Keene
posts.cooked: I’d go Chateauneuf but I’m biased.

posts.name: Brian G r a f s t r o m
posts.cooked:
Rose — an assertive one, ideally.

IMO, a dry red would be terrible with this dish. A white would be better, and one with a little bit of r.s. might be best.

But do the rose if you can.

ETA: Now that I’ve gone back and read this thread I see @Sarah_Kirschbaum and @DougS already made my points for me (when I said white with a little bit of r.s. a demi-sec Vouvray was exactly what I had in mind (Huet demi, to be even more precise), but I chose to keep things general, per your request).
posts.display_username: Brian G r a f s t r o m

posts.name: Ph!l M@rx
posts.cooked: Dinner postponed until Sunday so I have more time for suggestions and a shopping trip to acquire something that is not in my cellar. Overall, the menu is French oriented (pates, escargot, cheese plates, rabbit, etc. ) The Tagine I guess qualifies as French Colonial - I have had it before and it is very tasty.

posts.name: Karl K@zaks
posts.cooked: I like the stouter rose and Grenache suggestions.

posts.name: Zachary Pearson
posts.cooked:
Phil,

Etna Rosso, Cannonau di Sardegna, Corsican reds. I don’t think the harissa is going to play nicely with the high abv southern Rhones and this is lighter/more delicate anyways.

Thanks,

Zachary

posts.name: David Glasser
posts.cooked: +1 to Cote Rotie and big Chateauneuf suggestions.

posts.name: Craig Gleason
posts.cooked: If it’s not too spicy (hot) I’d be happy to drink almost anything with this.

posts.name: Nate Simon
posts.cooked: Grenache sounds like a great option.

posts.name: Robert M yers
posts.cooked: Tavel Rose?

a young (ish) Musar Blanc seems like it could stand up to the spices & flavors if you like esoteric whites. While you may be inclined to go red, I like the bold rose & Alsace thoughts mentioned above as well.

posts.name: Robert Dentice
posts.cooked: I think an orange wine would go excellent with this dish. It has the tannin and structure to stand up to the meet and the spice to play with the african spices. Radikon or Gravner preferably.

posts.name: John Davis
posts.cooked: Agree with N. Rhône and Grenache.

posts.name: Richard Flack
posts.cooked: Gravonia? I’m assuming the lamb is cooked medium not rare with that spicing.

posts.name: Ph!l M@rx
posts.cooked:
My current lineup (which hopefully will also go nicely with my companions’ entree choices ):
‘20 Roger Sabon CdP cuvee Prestige and a ‘21 Fingers Crossed Unanswered Prayers Rose. Might bring a Syrah heavy wine as a 3rd option

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: I actually tried a few Moroccan wines earlier this year, which were pretty decent (they have some latitude to use imported wine, mainly from Spain, in their blends, so you have to pay attention) and went well with tajine.

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked:
A question for @Phil_M is whether he wants to bring a more significant and profound wine for the occasion, or just wants any wine that pairs most easily with the dish, including maybe simple roses or whites.

That helps shape the issue some and which directions to look.

posts.name: Sarah Kirschbaum
posts.cooked:
Chris_Seiber:

A question for @Phil_M is whether he wants to bring a more significant and profound wine for the occasion, or just wants any wine that pairs most easily with the dish, including maybe simple roses or whites.

That helps shape the issue some and which directions to look.

This is such a good point, Chris. Those considerations are real. We have a definitive preference for best match over significance or impact, but have for sure taken other elements into consideration.

posts.name: Dan Kravitz
posts.cooked:
I like a lot of the suggestions and I believe that Tajine welcomes a wide variety.

My first choice would be a bold Rose, not light in color or body, Greek or Spanish, maybe southern Italian.

I’m OK with Northern Rhone but Nebbiolo or Xynomavro should be fine. I would not go to an RS white, but could see one at the threshhold… Alsace Pinot Gris. I would not go Chateauneuf, too much sumo wrestling there.

posts.name: Adam Frisch
posts.cooked: Well, a White Zinfandel, of course!

posts.name: Ph!l M@rx
posts.cooked:
The 2 wines we ended up opening - the Roger Sabon CdP and the Rose were excellent and paired nicely with both the lamb dish and the other entree selections. The Rose in particular was full-bodied for a Rose and could stand up to the Moroccan spices without overwhelming them.
To Chris- this was a get together with good friends who are our regular dining companions; so while we wanted something nice, we were not looking for a special occasion type experience.
Thanks to all for the many excellent suggestions and a lively dialog!

posts.name: Dan Kravitz
posts.cooked:
Phil_M:

the Roger Sabon CdP and the Rose

Which Rose?

Inquiring minds want to know!

posts.name: Sarah Kirschbaum
posts.cooked: Bravo. Rosé can be a genius and, to me, essential pairing tool for those who can think of it beyond something simple to sip on the deck. It’s amusing that many who can take rosé champagne seriously, can’t find room for respecting still examples.

fancy_title: What wine would you have with this dish?
title: What wine would you have with this dish?
